inline_review:
commit e5886df8568874238aeaad77764ac8d57b575f7c
Author: Zhaoyang Huang <zhaoyang.huang@unisoc.com>

mm: skip dirty file folios during isolation of legacy LRU

This commit attempts to bring dirty file folios detection and statistics
forward to the isolation phase in legacy LRU. It aims to skip dirty file
folios to improve scan efficiency and decrease unnecessary TLB flushes.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> index 605cae534bf8..1a8e822054c8 100644
> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c
[ ... ]
> @@ -1725,6 +1707,30 @@ static unsigned long isolate_lru_folios(unsigned long nr_to_scan,
>  		 */
>  		scan += nr_pages;
>  
> +		if (!folio_trylock(folio))
> +			goto move;

Does this unconditionally skip any locked folio, including clean or anonymous
ones? 

If the goto move path moves the folio back to the source list, could this
disrupt LRU ordering by treating temporarily locked folios as recently used?
If the tail of the LRU contains many locked folios, skipping them might cause
the upper reclaim logic to falsely assume no progress is being made.

> +		/*
> +		 * The number of dirty pages determines if a node is marked
> +		 * reclaim_congested. kswapd will stall and start writing
> +		 * folios if the tail of the LRU is all dirty unqueued folios.
> +		 */
> +		folio_check_dirty_writeback(folio, &dirty, &writeback);
> +		folio_unlock(folio);
> +
> +		if (dirty || writeback)
> +			nr_dirty += nr_pages;
> +
> +		if (dirty && !writeback)
> +			nr_unqueued_dirty += nr_pages;
> +		/*
> +		 * Treat this folio as congested if folios are cycling
> +		 * through the LRU so quickly that the folios marked
> +		 * for immediate reclaim are making it to the end of
> +		 * the LRU a second time.
> +		 */
> +		if (writeback && folio_test_reclaim(folio))
> +			nr_congested += nr_pages;
> +
>  		if (!folio_test_lru(folio))
>  			goto move;
>  		if (!sc->may_unmap && folio_mapped(folio))
>  			goto move;

Are these reclaim statistics artificially inflated? 

The counters are incremented before checking folio_test_lru() and 
folio_try_get(). If isolation fails, the unisolated folios will still be 
added to sc->nr.* at the end of the function, which might corrupt the metrics 
used by kswapd to detect node congestion.

Also, the commit message mentions skipping dirty file folios, but after 
calculating these statistics, the code falls through to the existing 
isolation checks. Is there a missing check to actually skip them, so they 
are not passed to shrink_folio_list()?

[ ... ]
> @@ -1774,6 +1780,35 @@ static unsigned long isolate_lru_folios(unsigned long nr_to_scan,
>  	trace_mm_vmscan_lru_isolate(sc->reclaim_idx, sc->order, nr_to_scan,
>  				    total_scan, skipped, nr_taken, lru);
>  	update_lru_sizes(lruvec, lru, nr_zone_taken);
> +	/*
> +	 * If dirty folios are scanned that are not queued for IO, it
> +	 * implies that flushers are not doing their job. This can
> +	 * happen when memory pressure pushes dirty folios to the end of
> +	 * the LRU before the dirty limits are breached and the dirty
> +	 * data has expired. It can also happen when the proportion of
> +	 * dirty folios grows not through writes but through memory
> +	 * pressure reclaiming all the clean cache. And in some cases,
> +	 * the flushers simply cannot keep up with the allocation
> +	 * rate. Nudge the flusher threads in case they are asleep.
> +	 */
> +	if (nr_unqueued_dirty == scan) {
> +		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
> +		/*
> +		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
> +		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
> +		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
> +		 *
> +		 * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly
> +		 * enough for cgroupv1 writeback throttling to work
> +		 * on a large system.
> +		 */
> +		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
> +			reclaim_throttle(lruvec_pgdat(lruvec), VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
> +	}

Can nr_unqueued_dirty == scan ever be true in practice? 

The scan variable is incremented for every folio in the loop, but 
nr_unqueued_dirty is only incremented for successfully trylocked dirty 
folios. If any folio is skipped because it is clean, locked, or unmapped, 
scan will be strictly greater than nr_unqueued_dirty. This effectively 
disables flusher thread wakeups and writeback throttling.

Furthermore, isolate_lru_folios() is called from shrink_inactive_list() with 
the lruvec_lock_irq spinlock held, which disables interrupts:

shrink_inactive_list()
  lruvec_lock_irq(lruvec);
  isolate_lru_folios()
    reclaim_throttle()

Since reclaim_throttle() defines a wait queue and explicitly calls 
schedule_timeout(), does calling it here cause the task to sleep while 
holding a spinlock with interrupts disabled? This might trigger a scheduling 
while atomic regression under memory pressure.
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Calling blocking functions `reclaim_throttle()` and `wakeup_flusher_threads()` inside an IRQ-disabled spinlock critical section.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. `isolate_lru_folios()` is called from `shrink_inactive_list()` with the `lruvec_lock_irq` spinlock held (which disables interrupts).
2. The patch adds calls to `reclaim_throttle()` and `wakeup_flusher_threads()` directly inside this function.
3. `reclaim_throttle()` defines a wait queue and explicitly calls `schedule_timeout()`, sleeping the task.
4. Sleeping while holding a spinlock with interrupts disabled is a severe context violation that will immediately trigger a 'scheduling while atomic' BUG and cause kernel panics under memory pressure."},{"problem":"The flusher wakeup and throttling condition `nr_unqueued_dirty == scan` is broken by skipped folios.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The patch changes the writeback throttling condition to compare `nr_unqueued_dirty` against `scan` (the total number of folios evaluated).
2. `scan` is incremented for every folio in the loop. However, `nr_unqueued_dirty` is only incremented for dirty folios that successfully pass `folio_trylock()`.
3. If any folio is skipped (e.g., it is clean, locked, unmapped, not LRU, or fails `folio_try_get`), `scan` will be strictly greater than `nr_unqueued_dirty`.
4. This makes the condition practically impossible to satisfy, effectively disabling flusher thread wakeups and writeback throttling, which can lead to severe stalls or OOMs during heavy I/O."},{"problem":"Indiscriminate skipping of all locked folios disrupts LRU ordering and degrades reclaim.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The patch adds `if (!folio_trylock(folio)) goto move;` before any dirty or file checks. This unconditionally skips any locked folio, including clean or anonymous ones.
2. The `goto move` path uses `move_to = src`, which moves the folio to the head (most recently used end) of the LRU via `list_move(&folio->lru, src)`.
3. This breaks LRU ordering by treating any temporarily locked folio as recently used, shielding it from reclaim.
4. Furthermore, if the tail of the LRU contains many locked folios, skipping them causes `nr_taken` to be 0, making the upper reclaim logic falsely assume no progress and aggressively drop scan priority towards OOM."},{"problem":"The patch claims to skip dirty file folios but lacks the implementation to actually skip them.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. The commit message explicitly states the intent to 'skip dirty file folios during isolation'.
2. However, after calling `folio_check_dirty_writeback()` and updating the statistics, the code simply falls through to the existing isolation checks.
3. There is no conditional logic added (such as `if (dirty && folio_is_file_lru(folio)) goto move;`) to actually skip these folios.
4. As a result, they are still successfully isolated and passed to `shrink_folio_list()`, meaning the patch completely fails to implement its stated primary goal."},{"problem":"Reclaim writeback and congestion statistics are artificially inflated by unisolated folios.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. The patch increments `nr_dirty`, `nr_unqueued_dirty`, and `nr_congested` immediately after the trylock check.
2. Afterwards, the folio may still fail isolation checks like `!folio_test_lru(folio)`, `!sc->may_unmap`, or `!folio_try_get(folio)`.
3. Because these local counters are directly added to `sc->nr.*`, the vmscan statistics are artificially inflated with folios that were never actually isolated or processed by reclaim.
4. This corrupts the metrics used by `kswapd` to detect node congestion, potentially causing unnecessary stalls."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit e5886df8568874238aeaad77764ac8d57b575f7c
